clinicalStem: A 7-month-old male infant is brought to the emergency department by his mother who noticed he had a fever of 39.4°C, was inconsolably crying for 4 hours, and had two episodes of vomiting. On examination, he has a bulging anterior fontanelle, appears lethargic, and has a high-pitched cry. His neck is stiff. He is not fully vaccinated — he has not received the Hib or meningococcal vaccines. His capillary refill time is 3 seconds and he is pale. SpO₂ is 96% on room air. What is the MOST APPROPRIATE immediate next step?
options: Perform a lumbar puncture immediately to confirm the diagnosis before starting antibiotics
Administer IV ceftriaxone after securing IV access and stabilising the airway and circulation
Order a CT head before any other intervention to rule out raised intracranial pressure
Give oral paracetamol and observe for 2 hours to assess fever trajectory
Start oral amoxicillin and arrange outpatient MRI within 24 hours
explanation: This infant has classical signs of bacterial meningitis (bulging fontanelle, high-pitched cry, neck stiffness, fever, lethargy) with haemodynamic compromise (prolonged CRT, pallor). The priority is ABCDE stabilisation followed by immediate IV broad-spectrum antibiotics — ceftriaxone covers the most likely pathogens (Neisseria meningitidis, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Hib). Antibiotics must NOT be delayed for LP or CT when there are signs of sepsis or raised ICP.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Performing LP first is dangerous when there are signs of raised ICP (bulging fontanelle) and haemodynamic instability; it risks fatal brainstem herniation and must not delay antibiotics.
Correct. Stabilise (airway, IV access, fluids) then give IV ceftriaxone immediately — this is the life-saving priority in suspected bacterial meningitis with sepsis.
CT head should not precede antibiotics in a haemodynamically unstable infant with clinical meningitis; antibiotic administration must not be delayed for imaging.
Oral paracetamol and observation are completely inappropriate in a critically ill infant with meningitis signs and haemodynamic compromise.
Oral amoxicillin has no role in bacterial meningitis, and outpatient MRI is dangerous — this child requires emergency inpatient IV antibiotics.
clinicalPearl: In suspected bacterial meningitis with sepsis or raised ICP signs, give IV antibiotics IMMEDIATELY — do not delay for LP or CT.
keyClue: Bulging anterior fontanelle + high-pitched cry + neck stiffness + prolonged capillary refill time in an unvaccinated infant.
commonExamTrap: Performing LP before antibiotics — always treat first when there are signs of raised ICP or haemodynamic instability.
missedConcept: Antibiotic therapy must not be delayed for diagnostic procedures in meningitis with sepsis or raised ICP.
redFlagReason: Infant with bacterial meningitis and haemodynamic compromise (raised ICP, sepsis signs) — delayed antibiotics risk death or severe disability; LP in this setting risks fatal herniation.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old girl is brought to the ED with a 3-day history of fever (38.8°C), progressive behavioural change (aggression, confusion), and two witnessed seizures. Her parents report she had a cold sore on her lip 2 weeks ago. On examination she is disoriented, has focal weakness of the right arm, and an EEG shows periodic lateralised epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) over the left temporal lobe. CSF analysis shows: WBC 85 cells/mm³ (predominantly lymphocytes), protein 0.9 g/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L (serum 5.1 mmol/L). What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
options: Bacterial meningitis caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae
Tuberculous meningitis
Herpes simplex virus encephalitis
Autoimmune (anti-NMDAR) encephalitis
Enteroviral meningitis
explanation: HSV encephalitis classically presents with fever, behavioural change, focal neurological deficits, and temporal lobe involvement (PLEDs on EEG, temporal signal change on MRI). The history of recent cold sore (HSV-1 reactivation), lymphocytic pleocytosis with raised protein, and normal CSF:serum glucose ratio point strongly to HSV encephalitis. IV aciclovir should be started empirically without waiting for CSF PCR results.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Bacterial meningitis causes neutrophilic pleocytosis, very high protein, and low CSF glucose — this CSF is lymphocyte-predominant with normal glucose, inconsistent with bacterial meningitis.
Tuberculous meningitis is possible but typically has a more indolent onset, higher protein, lower glucose, and a different epidemiological context; PLEDs and rapid focal progression favour HSV.
Correct. Fever + behavioural change + focal deficits + temporal PLEDs + prior cold sore history + lymphocytic CSF = classic HSV encephalitis.
Anti-NMDAR encephalitis is a consideration for behavioural change but is typically afebrile or mildly febrile, has a more gradual onset, and is not linked to prior herpetic lesions.
Enteroviral meningitis is typically a benign self-limiting illness without focal neurological deficits or temporal EEG changes.
clinicalPearl: HSV encephalitis = fever + behavioural change + temporal lobe involvement (PLEDs/EEG) ± prior cold sore — start IV aciclovir empirically without waiting for PCR.
keyClue: Recent cold sore + behavioural change + focal deficits + temporal PLEDs on EEG + lymphocytic CSF.
commonExamTrap: Waiting for PCR results before starting aciclovir — empirical treatment must begin immediately given the high mortality of untreated HSV encephalitis.
missedConcept: HSV encephalitis preferentially involves the temporal lobe and requires urgent empirical aciclovir regardless of awaiting PCR confirmation.

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old boy is brought by ambulance after his mother noticed a rapidly spreading rash. He has a fever of 40.1°C, is irritable, and has a non-blanching purpuric rash on his trunk and legs. He is tachycardic (HR 165 bpm), his BP is 68/42 mmHg, and his capillary refill time is 5 seconds. The paramedics have already given him IM benzylpenicillin en route. Which of the following is the MOST APPROPRIATE immediate in-hospital management priority?
options: Lumbar puncture to confirm meningococcal meningitis before further antibiotics
Administer IV ceftriaxone, give fluid resuscitation with 10 mL/kg IV 0.9% saline bolus, and activate the sepsis pathway
Order blood cultures and wait for results before escalating antibiotic therapy
Start oral prednisolone to reduce inflammation from the purpuric rash
Arrange urgent CT head to look for cerebral involvement before further management
explanation: This child has meningococcal septicaemia with haemodynamic shock (hypotension, tachycardia, prolonged CRT, non-blanching purpuric rash). Following pre-hospital penicillin, in-hospital management requires immediate IV ceftriaxone (escalating from penicillin), fluid resuscitation with IV crystalloid boluses (10 mL/kg, repeated as needed), and activation of the sepsis/shock pathway. This is a life-threatening emergency requiring simultaneous resuscitation and antibiotics.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Lumbar puncture is absolutely contraindicated in haemodynamic shock and in the presence of a purpuric rash suggesting raised ICP and coagulopathy.
Correct. IV ceftriaxone + fluid resuscitation + sepsis pathway activation is the correct simultaneous management of meningococcal septicaemia with shock.
Waiting for blood culture results before escalating treatment wastes critical time in a child in septic shock — empirical antibiotics and resuscitation are the priority.
Oral prednisolone has no role in acute meningococcal septicaemia with shock; dexamethasone may be used adjunctively in meningitis but only IV and not as a first-line intervention here.
CT head is not the priority in a child in septic shock; airway, breathing, circulation, and antibiotics come first.
clinicalPearl: Non-blanching purpuric rash + fever + haemodynamic instability = meningococcal septicaemia — immediate IV antibiotics and fluid resuscitation, LP is contraindicated.
keyClue: Non-blanching purpuric rash + haemodynamic shock (hypotension, tachycardia, prolonged CRT) in a febrile toddler.
commonExamTrap: Performing LP in a shocked child with purpura — it is absolutely contraindicated; it must never delay resuscitation.
missedConcept: A non-blanching rash with haemodynamic compromise is meningococcal septicaemia until proven otherwise; immediate antibiotics and resuscitation override all diagnostics.
redFlagReason: Meningococcal septicaemia with haemodynamic shock — delayed treatment is rapidly fatal; LP in this setting is life-threatening.

clinicalStem: A 13-year-old obese boy (BMI 34 kg/m², 99th percentile for age) attends clinic for a routine review. He has acanthosis nigricans at the nape of his neck and axillae. His blood pressure is 138/88 mmHg. Fasting bloods show: glucose 6.4 mmol/L, triglycerides 2.8 mmol/L, HDL 0.8 mmol/L, and ALT 62 U/L. He snores loudly and his mother reports he sometimes stops breathing at night. Which complication of obesity is MOST CONSISTENT with the ALT elevation and the clinical findings?
options: Type 1 diabetes mellitus causing hepatitis
Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD)
Autoimmune hepatitis triggered by obesity
Coeliac disease with liver involvement
Viral hepatitis B acquired perinatally
explanation: NAFLD is the most common cause of elevated transaminases in obese children and adolescents. It is directly linked to insulin resistance (evidenced here by acanthosis nigricans, impaired fasting glucose, hypertriglyceridaemia, low HDL, and hypertension — components of metabolic syndrome). NAFLD can progress to NASH and cirrhosis. The metabolic context makes NAFLD overwhelmingly the most likely explanation for the ALT elevation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Type 1 diabetes causes autoimmune beta-cell destruction and does not cause hepatitis; this boy's glucose of 6.4 mmol/L suggests impaired fasting glucose consistent with insulin resistance, not T1DM.
Correct. NAFLD is the leading cause of elevated ALT in obese children with metabolic syndrome — insulin resistance drives hepatic steatosis and inflammation.
Autoimmune hepatitis is possible but would typically have higher ALT/AST, positive autoantibodies, and is not specifically associated with obesity or metabolic syndrome.
Coeliac disease can cause mild transaminase elevation but is not the predominant association with obesity, acanthosis nigricans, and dyslipidaemia.
Perinatal hepatitis B is possible in any child but is not linked to the metabolic syndrome pattern seen here; vaccination history should be checked but is not the most consistent explanation.
clinicalPearl: NAFLD is the most common liver disease in obese children — always screen with LFTs; elevated ALT in an obese child with metabolic syndrome = NAFLD until proven otherwise.
keyClue: Obese adolescent + acanthosis nigricans + dyslipidaemia + impaired fasting glucose + elevated ALT = metabolic syndrome with NAFLD.
commonExamTrap: Attributing elevated ALT to viral hepatitis without considering NAFLD in the context of obesity and metabolic syndrome.
missedConcept: NAFLD is a major complication of childhood obesity driven by insulin resistance and is closely linked to metabolic syndrome.

subtopic: Obstructive sleep apnoea as a complication
clinicalStem: A 10-year-old obese girl (BMI 31 kg/m², 98th percentile) is referred by her school because her teacher noticed she is falling asleep during class and is inattentive. Her mother reports loud snoring every night and witnessed pauses in breathing lasting several seconds. On examination, she has tonsillar enlargement (grade 3), a high-arched palate, and her neck circumference is at the 97th centile. Her parents are concerned about her tiredness despite a reportedly adequate sleep duration. What is the BEST INVESTIGATION to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Thyroid function tests to rule out hypothyroidism
Full polysomnography (overnight sleep study)
Lateral neck X-ray to assess adenoid size
Echocardiogram to assess for right heart disease
Fasting insulin and glucose tolerance test
explanation: The clinical picture strongly suggests obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA): obesity, tonsillar hypertrophy, witnessed apnoeas, snoring, and daytime somnolence/inattention. The gold standard investigation for OSA is overnight polysomnography, which quantifies the apnoea-hypopnoea index (AHI) and confirms the diagnosis. In children, an AHI ≥1 is considered abnormal.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Thyroid function tests are appropriate as a screen for hypothyroidism (which can cause daytime somnolence) but are not the investigation of choice to confirm OSA.
Correct. Overnight polysomnography is the gold standard for diagnosing OSA — it measures respiratory events, oxygen desaturation, and sleep architecture.
Lateral neck X-ray can suggest adenoid hypertrophy but does not diagnose or quantify OSA; it is not the gold-standard investigation.
Echocardiogram is relevant once OSA is established and pulmonary hypertension is suspected (cor pulmonale), but is not the first investigation to confirm OSA.
Fasting insulin and OGTT are appropriate for metabolic screening in obese children but do not diagnose OSA.
clinicalPearl: Obesity + snoring + witnessed apnoeas + daytime somnolence in a child = suspect OSA; confirm with overnight polysomnography (gold standard).
keyClue: Loud snoring + witnessed apnoeas + daytime sleepiness + tonsillar enlargement in an obese child.
commonExamTrap: Ordering lateral neck X-ray as the confirmatory test — it may suggest adenoid enlargement but cannot diagnose or quantify OSA.
missedConcept: OSA is a recognised complication of childhood obesity; polysomnography is the gold standard investigation.

clinicalStem: A 6-week-old female infant is brought in with a 10-day history of cough. The mother reports episodes of paroxysmal coughing followed by a high-pitched inspiratory 'whoop', post-tussive vomiting, and episodes of apnoea. The infant appears well between attacks. Temperature is 37.4°C. She has not received any vaccinations yet. FBC shows: WBC 28 × 10⁹/L with 90% lymphocytes. CXR shows no consolidation. What is the MOST LIKELY causative organism?
options: Mycoplasma pneumoniae
Bordetella pertussis
Respiratory syncytial virus
Staphylococcus aureus
Haemophilus influenzae type b
explanation: The classic triad of paroxysmal cough, inspiratory whoop, and post-tussive vomiting in an unvaccinated young infant, combined with a striking lymphocytosis on FBC, is diagnostic of Bordetella pertussis infection (whooping cough). The marked lymphocytosis (WBC >20 × 10⁹/L, predominantly lymphocytes) is a characteristic haematological finding of pertussis. Apnoea in young infants without the classic whoop is another presentation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Mycoplasma pneumoniae causes atypical pneumonia in older children (school-age), not a paroxysmal cough with lymphocytosis in a 6-week-old.
Correct. Bordetella pertussis causes whooping cough — classic features are paroxysmal cough + whoop + post-tussive vomiting + marked lymphocytosis in an unvaccinated infant.
RSV causes bronchiolitis with wheeze and respiratory distress, not paroxysmal cough with whooping and marked lymphocytosis.
Staphylococcus aureus causes pneumonia with cavitation/abscess; it does not cause the pertussis clinical syndrome.
Hib causes epiglottitis or meningitis; it does not cause the paroxysmal cough syndrome.
clinicalPearl: Paroxysmal cough + inspiratory whoop + post-tussive vomiting + lymphocytosis >20 × 10⁹/L in an unvaccinated infant = Bordetella pertussis until proven otherwise.
keyClue: Paroxysmal cough + whoop + post-tussive vomiting + WBC 28 × 10⁹/L with 90% lymphocytes in an unvaccinated 6-week-old.
commonExamTrap: Attributing the high WBC to bacterial infection requiring neutrophilia — pertussis characteristically causes marked lymphocytosis, not neutrophilia.
missedConcept: Pertussis causes a characteristic marked lymphocytosis; apnoea may be the only presentation in very young infants who may not produce the classic whoop.

subtopic: Clinical features and management in children
clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy returns from a 3-week visit to relatives in South Asia. He has had fever for 12 days, reaching 39.8°C each evening, with relative bradycardia (HR 72 bpm despite fever). He has mild splenomegaly, a faint rose-coloured rash on his trunk, and mild abdominal pain. He appears toxic. Blood culture from day 3 of fever grows a gram-negative organism confirmed as Salmonella typhi, which is reported as susceptible to ceftriaxone but showing intermediate resistance to ciprofloxacin. Which is the MOST APPROPRIATE antibiotic treatment?
options: Oral amoxicillin for 14 days
IV ceftriaxone for 10–14 days
Oral ciprofloxacin for 7 days
IV vancomycin for 10 days
IM gentamicin for 7 days
explanation: For typhoid fever caused by Salmonella typhi susceptible to ceftriaxone, IV ceftriaxone (or cefotaxime) is the preferred treatment in children, especially for severe disease in a toxic-appearing child. Given the intermediate resistance to fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin), that class should be avoided. Ceftriaxone achieves excellent intracellular penetration and is the standard of care for complicated or drug-resistant typhoid in children.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Amoxicillin resistance is common in Salmonella typhi worldwide; it is not recommended without confirmed sensitivity and is inappropriate for a toxic-appearing child with severe disease.
Correct. IV ceftriaxone is the drug of choice for typhoid in a severely ill child when the isolate is susceptible and fluoroquinolone resistance is present.
Ciprofloxacin is inappropriate here because the organism shows intermediate resistance, and fluoroquinolones are generally avoided in children due to concerns over cartilage toxicity.
Vancomycin has no activity against gram-negative organisms and has no role in typhoid fever.
Gentamicin has poor intracellular penetration and is not effective against intracellular Salmonella typhi; it is not used for typhoid.
clinicalPearl: For typhoid fever in children with fluoroquinolone resistance, IV ceftriaxone (10–14 days) is the preferred treatment — it achieves excellent tissue penetration against Salmonella typhi.
keyClue: Prolonged fever + relative bradycardia + rose spots + splenomegaly + return from endemic area + blood culture growing S. typhi susceptible to ceftriaxone.
commonExamTrap: Choosing ciprofloxacin despite reported intermediate resistance — always follow sensitivity results and note that fluoroquinolones are generally avoided in children.
missedConcept: Emerging fluoroquinolone resistance in S. typhi makes ceftriaxone the preferred agent for severe paediatric typhoid; relative bradycardia is a classic clinical clue.

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old boy is brought in after developing a large, tense haemarthrosis of his right knee following a minor fall. His mother states this is the third joint bleed this year and that his maternal uncle required 'blood transfusions' for similar problems. His FBC and platelet count are normal. Bleeding time is normal. PT is normal. APTT is markedly prolonged at 78 seconds (control 32 seconds). The APTT corrects fully on mixing study with normal plasma. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
options: Von Willebrand disease type 1
Haemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency)
Haemophilia B (Factor IX deficiency)
Disseminated intravascular coagulation
Bernard–Soulier syndrome
explanation: Haemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) is an X-linked recessive condition presenting in males with recurrent haemarthroses and deep tissue bleeds. The characteristic laboratory pattern is: normal platelet count, normal bleeding time, normal PT, prolonged APTT that corrects on mixing study (indicating factor deficiency, not inhibitor). Maternal uncle involvement on the mother's side confirms X-linked inheritance. Factor VIII deficiency is more common (1:5000 males) than Factor IX deficiency.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Von Willebrand disease typically has a prolonged bleeding time (due to platelet adhesion defect) and may have mildly prolonged APTT, but haemarthroses are rare — it is not the primary diagnosis here.

Correct. X-linked inheritance (maternal uncle) + haemarthroses + normal PT + prolonged APTT correcting on mixing study = Haemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) — the most common severe coagulation disorder.

Haemophilia B (Factor IX deficiency) is clinically indistinguishable from Haemophilia A but is less common (1:30000 males); specific factor assays are required to differentiate the two — Haemophilia A is statistically more likely.
DIC presents with prolonged PT and APTT, low fibrinogen, low platelets, and in the context of a precipitating illness — not in a well child with recurrent haemarthroses.

Bernard–Soulier syndrome is a platelet disorder with thrombocytopaenia (giant platelets), prolonged bleeding time, and mucocutaneous bleeding — not haemarthroses and not associated with prolonged APTT.
clinicalPearl: Recurrent haemarthroses + prolonged APTT correcting on mixing study + normal PT/platelets + X-linked family history = Haemophilia A until factor assays prove otherwise.
keyClue: Recurrent haemarthroses + maternal uncle affected + prolonged APTT correcting on mixing study + normal PT and platelets.
commonExamTrap: Choosing Haemophilia B over A — both present identically; A is far more common (6× more prevalent) and is statistically the better answer without factor assay results.
missedConcept: The mixing study differentiates factor deficiency (APTT corrects) from factor inhibitor (APTT does not correct); haemarthrosis is the hallmark bleed in severe haemophilia.

clinicalStem: A 5-year-old girl presents with a 3-day history of widespread bruising, petechiae on her legs, and two episodes of nosebleed lasting 5 minutes each. She had a viral upper respiratory tract infection 2 weeks ago. She is well-appearing, afebrile, and haemodynamically stable. FBC shows: Hb 12.1 g/dL, WBC 7.2 × 10⁹/L (normal differential), platelets 11 × 10⁹/L. Blood film shows thrombocytopaenia with large platelets but no blast cells. PT and APTT are normal. What is the MOST APPROPRIATE initial management?
options: Emergency platelet transfusion to raise the platelet count above 50 × 10⁹/L
Urgent bone marrow biopsy before starting any treatment
Monitor closely with safety netting, with consideration of IV immunoglobulin or oral corticosteroids given the low platelet count and active bleeding
Start high-dose methotrexate for presumed leukaemia
Immediate splenectomy as first-line definitive treatment
explanation: Childhood ITP typically follows a viral illness, presents with mucocutaneous bleeding, thrombocytopaenia with large platelets, and a normal blood film (no blasts). In a haemodynamically stable child with minor bleeding (epistaxis, bruising), management depends on severity: platelets <20 × 10⁹/L with bleeding warrants IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) or corticosteroids to rapidly raise the platelet count. Platelet transfusion is not first-line in ITP (platelets are rapidly destroyed); splenectomy is reserved for chronic refractory ITP.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Platelet transfusion is not first-line in ITP — transfused platelets are rapidly destroyed by anti-platelet antibodies; it is reserved for life-threatening bleeding.

Bone marrow biopsy is not routinely required before treating typical childhood ITP; it is only indicated if atypical features (fever, lymphadenopathy, organomegaly, anaemia, or blasts) suggest leukaemia.

Correct. A well-appearing child with platelet count of 11 × 10⁹/L and active (but minor) bleeding is managed with IVIG or corticosteroids after confirming typical ITP features; close monitoring and safety netting are essential.
Methotrexate is chemotherapy for leukaemia — there are no blast cells on the film and the clinical picture is typical ITP, not ALL.
Splenectomy is a last resort for chronic refractory ITP (>12 months, failed medical therapy) — never the first-line treatment in a newly presenting child.
clinicalPearl: Post-viral thrombocytopaenia with large platelets and no blasts = childhood ITP; IVIG or corticosteroids for platelet counts <20 × 10⁹/L with bleeding — platelet transfusion only for life-threatening haemorrhage.
keyClue: Post-viral onset + isolated thrombocytopaenia + large platelets + no blasts + well-appearing child = ITP.
commonExamTrap: Ordering bone marrow biopsy before starting treatment — this is only needed if atypical features suggest leukaemia; typical childhood ITP does not require it.
missedConcept: In ITP, platelet transfusion is not first-line; anti-platelet antibodies destroy transfused platelets rapidly — IVIG or steroids are the preferred treatment.

clinicalStem: An 18-month-old boy is brought to clinic because his mother is worried he looks pale. She reports he drinks approximately 700 mL of cow's milk per day and eats very little solid food. He has not had any bleeding episodes. On examination he is pale and irritable, HR 108 bpm. FBC: Hb 8.2 g/dL, MCV 62 fL, MCH 18 pg, RDW elevated. Blood film shows microcytic hypochromic red cells with pencil cells. Serum ferritin is 4 μg/L (normal 12–300 μg/L). What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
options: Beta-thalassaemia major
Anaemia of chronic disease
Iron deficiency anaemia
Lead poisoning
Sideroblastic anaemia
explanation: This toddler has the classic picture of nutritional iron deficiency anaemia: excessive cow's milk intake (displaces iron-rich solids and inhibits iron absorption), microcytic hypochromic anaemia with elevated RDW, pencil cells on film, and a markedly low serum ferritin. Excessive milk intake is the most common cause of iron deficiency in toddlers in developed countries. Ferritin <12 μg/L confirms iron depletion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Beta-thalassaemia major presents in infancy with severe anaemia, massive splenomegaly, and characteristic target cells/nucleated RBCs on film; ferritin is normal or elevated, not depleted.
Anaemia of chronic disease is typically normocytic or mildly microcytic with normal or elevated ferritin and is not associated with excessive milk intake in an otherwise well toddler.
Correct. Excessive cow's milk + microcytic hypochromic anaemia + low ferritin + elevated RDW + pencil cells = nutritional iron deficiency anaemia in a toddler.

Lead poisoning causes microcytic anaemia with basophilic stippling on film and raised blood lead levels; it is in the differential but ferritin is not typically this low, and the history of excessive milk is more specific.
Sideroblastic anaemia is rare in children, usually hereditary or acquired, and characterised by ring sideroblasts on bone marrow examination — not the picture here.
clinicalPearl: Excessive cow's milk intake is the most common cause of iron deficiency in toddlers — milk is iron-poor, inhibits iron absorption, and displaces iron-rich foods.
keyClue: 18-month-old + 700 mL cow's milk/day + low solid food intake + microcytic hypochromic anaemia + ferritin 4 μg/L.
commonExamTrap: Choosing beta-thalassaemia — it has low ferritin vs normal/elevated ferritin; the combination of dietary history and very low ferritin distinguishes iron deficiency.
missedConcept: Cow's milk is the classic culprit in toddler iron deficiency: it is low in iron, inhibits absorption, and crowds out iron-rich complementary foods.

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old girl with known sickle cell disease (HbSS) presents to the ED after her mother noticed sudden onset pallor, lethargy, and a rapidly enlarging abdomen over the past 2 hours. On examination, she is pale, tachycardic (HR 178 bpm), and lethargic. Her spleen is palpable 6 cm below the left costal margin — it was 2 cm at her last visit 3 weeks ago. Her BP is 74/40 mmHg. FBC: Hb 3.8 g/dL (baseline 9.5 g/dL), platelets 88 × 10⁹/L. Reticulocyte count is 18%. What is the MOST APPROPRIATE immediate management?
options: Arrange urgent abdominal ultrasound to confirm splenic enlargement before intervention
Give IV morphine for pain and arrange splenectomy within 24 hours
Urgent red cell transfusion to restore circulating volume and haemoglobin, with resuscitation
Start hydroxyurea and discharge with haematology follow-up in 1 week
Perform exchange transfusion as the first intervention before any other treatment
explanation: Acute splenic sequestration crisis (ASSC) in sickle cell disease is a life-threatening emergency where massive amounts of blood pool in the spleen causing acute anaemia and circulatory shock. The hallmarks are: sudden drop in Hb (>2 g/dL from baseline), rapidly enlarging spleen, and haemodynamic compromise. Immediate management is emergency red cell transfusion to restore circulating volume and haemoglobin. Small volumes (5 mL/kg) may dramatically release sequestered blood from the spleen.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Ultrasound is not needed to confirm splenic enlargement — the diagnosis is clinical (rapidly enlarging spleen + acute Hb drop); imaging must not delay life-saving transfusion.
Morphine and delayed splenectomy are not appropriate first steps — haemodynamic shock must be corrected urgently with transfusion before any surgical consideration.

Correct. Emergency red cell transfusion restores circulating volume and corrects the life-threatening anaemia; avoid over-transfusion (target Hb just above baseline) as release of sequestered cells can cause hyperviscosity.
Hydroxyurea is a disease-modifying agent used for prevention of sickling crises — it has no role in the acute management of an ASSC with haemodynamic shock.
Exchange transfusion is used for acute chest syndrome and stroke — simple transfusion is the first-line treatment for ASSC; exchange transfusion may be considered later if indicated.
clinicalPearl: ASSC = sickle cell disease + sudden massive spleen enlargement + acute Hb drop + shock — immediate simple red cell transfusion is life-saving; use small volumes (5 mL/kg) and avoid Hb overshoot.
keyClue: HbSS + suddenly enlarging spleen + Hb drop >5 g/dL from baseline + haemodynamic shock + elevated reticulocytes = acute splenic sequestration crisis.
commonExamTrap: Choosing exchange transfusion as the first intervention — simple transfusion is first-line for ASSC; exchange transfusion is reserved for stroke and acute chest syndrome.
missedConcept: Acute splenic sequestration crisis requires immediate red cell transfusion; releasing sequestered blood during transfusion can overshoot Hb — target just above baseline to avoid hyperviscosity.
redFlagReason: Acute splenic sequestration crisis causes haemodynamic shock from sudden massive blood pooling in the spleen — delayed transfusion is rapidly fatal in young children with HbSS disease.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old boy of West African origin presents with a 2-day history of jaundice, pallor, and dark ('cola-coloured') urine following treatment with primaquine for malaria 5 days ago. His Hb is 6.1 g/dL (baseline ~11.5 g/dL), reticulocyte count is 14%, bilirubin is raised (predominantly unconjugated), and LDH is markedly elevated. Blood film shows bite cells and Heinz bodies on supravital staining. Which of the following BEST explains the mechanism of haemolysis in this patient?
options: Autoimmune destruction of red cells by IgG antibodies directed against red cell membrane antigens
Complement-mediated intravascular haemolysis triggered by membrane attack complex activation
Accumulation of oxidative stress causing denaturation of haemoglobin and red cell membrane damage due to G6PD deficiency
Sickling of haemoglobin S in low-oxygen environments causing vascular occlusion
Microangiopathic destruction of red cells in small vessels due to fibrin deposition
explanation: G6PD deficiency is an X-linked condition common in West African males. G6PD is essential for the hexose monophosphate shunt, producing NADPH which maintains glutathione in its reduced state — the main red cell antioxidant. Oxidant drugs (primaquine, dapsone, nitrofurantoin) and infection overwhelm the deficient G6PD pathway, leading to glutathione depletion, haemoglobin oxidation and denaturation (Heinz bodies), and red cell membrane damage (bite cells/blister cells), causing acute intravascular haemolysis with haemoglobinuria (dark urine).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia (IgG-mediated) is the mechanism in warm AIHA; it would show a positive direct antiglobulin test (Coombs test), not bite cells and Heinz bodies.
Complement-mediated haemolysis (MAC) is the mechanism in paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria (PNH) — not in G6PD deficiency triggered by an oxidant drug.
Correct. G6PD deficiency → NADPH depletion → glutathione depletion → oxidative damage → haemoglobin denaturation (Heinz bodies) → bite cells → intravascular haemolysis with haemoglobinuria.
HbS sickling causes vascular occlusion and haemolysis in sickle cell disease, not bite cells or Heinz bodies — the mechanism and morphology are different.
Microangiopathic haemolysis (in TTP/HUS/DIC) causes schistocytes on film, not bite cells and Heinz bodies, and occurs in a systemic thrombotic context.
clinicalPearl: G6PD deficiency haemolysis = oxidant drug exposure + bite cells + Heinz bodies + haemoglobinuria in an African or Mediterranean male — NADPH deficiency is the underlying mechanism.
keyClue: West African boy + primaquine exposure + bite cells + Heinz bodies + haemoglobinuria = G6PD deficiency oxidative haemolysis.
commonExamTrap: Confusing G6PD haemolysis (bite cells/Heinz bodies) with sickle cell crisis — the morphology and trigger are different; sickling does not produce bite cells.
missedConcept: G6PD is the rate-limiting enzyme of the hexose monophosphate shunt; its deficiency prevents NADPH generation, depleting antioxidant defences and making RBCs vulnerable to oxidative haemolysis.
